dialogue.utterance: Unit 3, respond to Southside for shortness of breath.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
She was sitting on her bed struggling to breathe and looked very distressed.
How long has this been going on?
About 30 minutes. We tried the nebulizer, but nothing seems to help.
Ma'am, can you hear me? Nod your head if you understand, okay?
(Nods slowly)
Can you tell me what day it is?
She can't really speak with this kind of breathing. She just nods yes or no.
Good, she's squeezing my hand - okay, we can work with that.
Is there any hoarseness or unusual sounds in her throat?
Just labored breathing. No gagging or strange noises.
Her breathing is fast and heavy.
Yes, her O2 saturation is 98% now, and she has a respiratory rate of 23.
The airway is maintainable. No signs of obstruction.
Do you notice any bluish tinge?
No, nothing like that.
Her pulse is 121, and BP is 197 over 113.
Does she have a history of asthma or breathing issues before?
Yes, she has severe asthma and COPD. She was in the hospital earlier for a headache and stomach problem.
Was she doing anything when the breathing problems started?
She was just sitting on the bed when it came on.
We also noted on her chart she has a history of congestive heart failure.
She's not showing signs of pain, but she is in distress from breathing.
Her lung sounds are diminished bilaterally. She also has frothy sputum in her mouth.
Can I pull back the blanket to look more closely at her breath patterns?
Sure, nothing seems unusual - just what we told you.
Alright, we'll take note. We would have liked to check for other signs of distress.
We tried drawing blood for the glucose, but the test didn't work.
Her breathing rate has dropped a bit to 21.
Her oxygen level is 99% now and she seems more stable.
Her heart rhythm has changed to sinus tachycardia - we'll monitor it closely.
I'm going to apply a 4-lead cardiac monitor now for continuous reading.
Monitor shows sinus rhythm with a rhythm strip.
Administering nebulizer with Albuterol and Atrovent now to help with her airway.
She's also receiving oral Dexamethasone now for inflammation control.
IV access was attempted, but not successful with this line.
Based on her vitals and condition, we're following protocol 3-11 for acute breathing difficulty due to asthma and CHF.
I'll be monitoring her every few minutes as she moves to the ambulance.
She's too weak to stand. We'll need to assist with moving her.
We can help. She's been quite weak the whole time.
[COMPANY_NAME] hospital has been selected for transport - they can handle the level of care she needs.
We agree. She's in no condition to wait any longer.
We'll hoist her into the upright sitting position now for transport. Ready, partner?
She's secured - comfortable and ready for the ambulance.
The CPAP is in place. We'll keep her oxygen level stable during the ride.
O2 remains steady at 99%, her vitals are doing well for now.
Medication stays onboard. CPAP is active.
Is there any new issue that could affect her condition right now?
No, nothing recent. Just the labored breathing and frothy sputum.
Has her breathing worsened since we started treatments?
She's still struggling, but the CPAP has appeared to ease the labored breathing.
We'll be keeping you updated throughout the drive over. The nurse at the hospital is ready.
We've got a complete set of data we'll hand over to the medical team at the hospital.
Vitals updated and synchronized. We generated a full report for the emergency department.
The EKG was updated and her heart is currently stable but continually under observation.
She was transported in a two-person lift and placed in a seated position for transit.
The 12-lead EKG also shows sinus tachycardia with some T-wave irregular findings.
She remains at 99%, pulsations are steady.
We'll continue with CPAP and reassess for improved responsiveness or any complications.
Just double-checking in case there was something we missed about her condition earlier.
Nothing else reported that we know of.
Any changes in how she's responding to treatments, or is she still clearly distressed?
She just nods, but visibly, the distress is no less than before.
She'll be handed directly to the ER nurse in the medical bay at [COMPANY_NAME].
GCS was noted on scene at 11 (4 for eyes, 1 for verbal, 6 for motor). Minimal verbal interaction followed.
All the medications given were per protocol, and no adverse reactions were observed.
Noted vitals for benchmarking.
Blood glucose couldn't be obtained in field and en route, but no insulin dependence was documented.
Clear documentation of her prior medication history, known allergens also considered with caution.
Her CPAP and attempt at IV are fully recorded and will be shared with the hospital team.
She was transported safely and maintained stable vitals with low effects to procedures.
Head-up positioning and multiple rechecks show no fluctuations in her current airway security.
Having high BP with tachycardia necessitated continuous monitoring for CHF and respiratory compromise.
All changes in mental status or EKG readings will serve as red flags if secondary assessment shows instability.
Ongoing planned care target is achieving stable cardiopulmonary scores in emergent transport.
No acquired or traumatic injuries noted during movement and constant interaction data considered.
The patient is receiving high-flow CPAP and maintaining lung function.
The presence of frothy sputum aligns with CHF exacerbation, and all signs are consistent with this.
Final destination slated for ECG interpretation team at [COMPANY_NAME] with pre-clearance of ED MCV.
Medications and vital updates fully and EMS platform records ready.
Transport anticipated as smooth and fully metric with repeat postmeals verification as needed.
No transport-specific interventions required after field assessments and stabilizing undertaken.
All EKG strips and glucose diary noted for arriving cardiology evaluation.
The Glasgow score maintained steady with no mental decline during transit.
Reassessments showed no fluctuations in baseline conditions.
No adverse symptoms emerged after any of the non-narcotic treatments initiated per protocol 3-11.
Patient's posture is ideal and en route with oxygen clearly structured from 911 onset.
Mid-transit reassessments noted slight BP drop and controlled tachycardia.
No incidents during physical maneuvers or transitions.
All airway maneuvers and medication instructions are recorded.
Initial plan included ventilatory support and cardiac monitoring during transit.
Any new complications will be communicated to the hospital nurse.
Transported sit ready, no downtime projected.
Dexamethasone and beta-agonists are used to manage the acute phase.
ED team expects baseline respiratory support.
The timeline shows a clear need for rapid transfer.
Patient responses to commands were minimal, as expected.
No major issues in transport and consistent signs during transit.
Complete medical handover, with no loss of medical data.
